summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – ADOPTION – Whether leave to commence adoption proceedings is in the best interests of the children – “prescribed adopting parent” – Leave granted under s 60G of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) to commence proceedings

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J

The first applicant and second applicant apply for leave to commence adoption proceedings in respect of X, born in 2009 pursuant to s 60G(1) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).
X resides with her mother (the second applicant), her stepfather (the first applicant), and her half-sibling, the son of the applicants.

Dispensing with service

The respondent to the proceedings, Mr Avitus, is X’s biological father and is a Chinese national. Ordinarily he would be served with the Initiating Application and all of the supporting documents as a matter of procedural fairness. However, the applicants seek an order dispensing with service of this application and supporting documents upon him pursuant to r 2.34(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).
The second applicant deposes that she and the respondent formed a relationship in 2008 and married in 2009 in Country C where they then lived. They divorced in Country C in 2011 and the respondent returned to live in China. According to the second applicant, apart from three small payments immediately after the divorce, he has provided no financial support for X since then.
The second applicant and X moved to live in New Zealand in late 2012. Between then and the end of 2014, X had some telephone contact with the respondent. By implication then, the second applicant had contact details for the respondent. She says, however, that since late 2014, X has had no contact with the respondent.
The second applicant swears that she has made various attempts to locate or contact the respondent over the last ten years with no success. She has spoken to his family apparently, who have undertaken to pass on messages, but she has not heard from him. She says that over time, she has lost all contact with anyone who knows him. Further, she swears that in 2020 she emailed him at his last known email address to check in on him during the COVID-19 pandemic. She received no response to that email, or another sent later that same year.
The second applicant emailed the respondent on 16 September 2025, at his last known email address and requested his consent to X’s adoption by the first respondent. She has received no reply.
In these circumstances, I consider that it is appropriate to make an order dispensing with service upon the respondent of the Initiating Application filed on 6 November 2025 and all supporting documents filed therewith.

Leave to apply for adoption

The process of adoption is governed by adoption legislation enacted by the legislatures of the state and territory governments in the Commonwealth of Australia. In the state of Queensland, the process of adoption is governed by the Adoption Act 2009 (Qld). However, a person may only apply to arrange an adoption of a stepchild if that person has been granted leave under the s 60G(1) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) to make an adoption application in respect of that child.
In Denforth & Sable [2023] FedCFamC1F 903, Carew J set out the less than straight forward statutory arrangements by which this court comes to have jurisdiction in such applications:
[3] Since 1 September 2021, the jurisdiction of this Court to determine an application for leave to commence adoption proceedings is dependent upon the proceedings being transferred from an inferior court, namely the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (formerly known as the Federal Circuit Court of Australia), a court of record, to this Court (formerly known as the Family Court of Australia), a superior court of record (see s 9(1)(a) and s 10(1)(a) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) (“FCFCOA Act”)). Since 1 September 2021, the proceedings can no longer be instituted in this Court, but because of a protocol between the Courts, such applications are generally transferred, such is the importance given to the jurisdiction relating to adoption.
[4] Section 60G of the FLA relevantly provides as follows:
(1) Subject to subsection (2), the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), ... may grant leave for proceedings to be commenced for the adoption of a child by a prescribed adopting parent.

(2) In proceedings for leave under subsection (1), the court must consider whether granting leave would be in the child’s best interests, having regard to the effect of paragraph 60F(4)(a), or paragraph 60HA(3)(a), and of sections 61E and 65J.
[5] Section 25 of the FCFCOA Act relevantly provides as follows:
(1) The Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) has original jurisdiction:
(a) if a matter, being the subject of a family law or child support proceeding, is transferred to the Court by the Court under section 51—as set out in paragraphs 132(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d); or

(b) if a matter, being the subject of a family law or child support proceeding, is transferred to the Court by the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) under section 149—as set out in paragraphs 132(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d); or

(c) as is conferred on the Court, or in respect of which proceedings may be instituted in the Court, by any other Act.

[6] A “family law or child support proceeding” is defined in s 7 of the FCFCOA Act and means “proceedings in respect of which the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) has original jurisdiction under section 132.”

[7] The original jurisdiction of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) relevantly includes “matters in respect of which proceedings may be instituted under the Family Law Act 1975”.

The present application was commenced in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on 6 November 2025 upon the first and second applicants filing an Initiating Application. It was transferred to this court and listed before me on 24 February 2026 by an order made by a Senior Judicial Registrar on 10 February 2026 pursuant to his delegated powers stipulated under the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) sch 2, item 29.2A.
I am satisfied that I am seized of jurisdiction to determine this application.
The discretion reposed in the court by s 60G(1) of the Act is enlivened when the application is made by a prescribed adopting parent. A prescribed adopting parent, in relation to a child, means: a parent of the child; or the spouse of, or a person in a de facto relationship with, a parent of the child; or a parent of the child and either his or her spouse or a person in a de facto relationship with the parent. The second applicant is a parent of the child and the first applicant is her spouse (see the definition of prescribed adopting parent under section 4(1) of the Act). I am satisfied that the first and second applicants are prescribed adopting parents.
In proceedings for leave under s 60G(1), the court must consider whether granting leave would be in the child’s best interests, having regard to the effect of s 60F(4)(a), or s 60HA(3)(a), and of ss 61E and 65J of the Act (see s 60G(2) of the Act).
Section 60F(4)(a) provides that a child who would otherwise be considered a child of a marriage as prescribed by s 60F(1)(a) of the Act, is no longer a child of that marriage where the child is adopted and leave under s 60G(1) of Act was granted in respect of that child. As the second applicant and the respondent were married at the time of X’s birth, she is a child of their marriage. If I grant leave to adopt and she is adopted by the first applicant, she will cease to be a child of the marriage between the second applicant and the respondent. I take that into account.
Section 60H(3)(a) has no application here because it applies where the parents of the child sought to be adopted were not married but in a de facto relationship (see s 60HA(1) of the Act).
Section 61E deals with the effect of adoption upon a person’s parental responsibility for the child adopted. If there has been a grant of leave under s 60G(2) of the Act then the effect of an adoption will be to remove from a person who had parental responsibility for the child adopted, that parental responsibility. A literal reading of s 61E(2) of the Act would mean that in this case, the second applicant’s and the respondent’s parental responsibility for X will end upon her adoption by the first applicant (see Cahill & Fryar [2008] FamCA 1245 at [13], Brock & Brock [2007] FamCA 1594 at [12], Tanner & Cape [2011] FamCA 665 at [15] and the other cases referred to therein). Despite the text of s 61E(2), in Hanlon & Hanlon [2022] FedCFamC1F 435, Gill J concluded that on its proper construction, s 61E of the Act did not operate to strip parental responsibility from the parent who was partnered with the adopting step-parent (see also Haskova & Viraj [2025] FedCFamC1F 103, at [19]).
I accept that the views expressed in Hanlon and Haskova are the preferable views of the work done by s 61E(2) of the Act and in the event that X is adopted by the first applicant, only the respondent’s parental responsibility for her will end, not that of the second applicant. I take that into account.
There is no parenting order made under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) in place for X. Section 65J of the Act does not arise for consideration.
The first applicant was born in 1970 and is currently 55 years of age. He is an Australian citizen, currently resides in Australia, but works in New Zealand on a fly in/fly out basis in the aviation industry.
The second applicant was born in 1975 and is currently 50 years of age. She was born in China and has also resided in Country C. She is a New Zealand citizen and currently resides in Australia.
The applicants commenced a relationship in 2017 and married in March 2019 in New Zealand. The first and second applicants and X have lived as a “family unit” since 2019, and with X’s half-brother Y since his birth in 2020. The family moved from New Zealand to Australia in 2025. They intend to reside permanently in Australia.
The first and second applicants provide for and attend to X’s developmental, psychological and emotional needs and the first applicant deposes that he and X have a close father-daughter bond.
X is currently at high school, is doing very well in her studies and participates in various extracurricular activities. With the support of the applicants X competed at an international level representing New Zealand in sports.
X is now 16 years of age. Both applicants depose to X’s wish to be adopted by the first applicant, a wish the second applicant says she has been expressing “for several years”. The second applicant swears that X recognises the first applicant as her father and that she wishes for her legal status to reflect the reality of her close relationship with him.

Disposition

Having regard to the above matters, I consider that leave to commence adoption proceedings should be granted and will so order.
